turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've had a persistent cough for about 5 days now. It started out dry, but it's become productive with yellowish-green sputum. I'm getting worried because it's not going away and I'm having trouble sleeping at night due to the coughing.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've just felt really miserable with this cough. It's not just the coughing itself, but also the fatigue and malaise that comes with it. I've missed three days of work already because I'm just too exhausted to get out of bed, and my students are always so energetic, it's hard for me to keep up even on good days.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: When I'm coughing, I get a bit short of breath and wheezy. It feels like my chest is tight too.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: Yes, actually, I've been experiencing night sweats. For the past 4 nights, I've woken up with my pajamas completely soaked through, which is really unusual for me because I'm not someone who usually sweats a lot at night.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, actually, it's just the night sweats that are concerning me. During the day when I'm experiencing symptoms, I might feel clammy or warm to the touch, but I don't really sweat excessively. It's only at night when I wake up with my pajamas soaked through that I notice significant sweating.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The cough and wheezing seem to be centered in my chest, but sometimes it feels like it radiates up into my throat. When I'm coughing, I get a bit of a sharp pain in my side too, just below my ribs, which can be pretty uncomfortable.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: Honestly, no, it doesn't seem to radiate that far. The discomfort and pain tend to stay in my chest and throat area when I'm coughing.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I don't think so. To be honest, I haven't really noticed any numbness or tingling in my arms during these episodes. But like you said earlier, this is all still pretty new to me and I'm not sure what's normal or not.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, actually. Like I said before, it seems like everything stays in my chest and throat area when I'm experiencing symptoms. I haven't really noticed anything out of the ordinary with my arms, legs, hands, or feet.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: I don't think so... I mean, I haven't really looked for any swelling, but nothing comes to mind. To be honest, my main concern has been getting rid of this cough and figuring out what's causing it, not checking for swelling or anything like that.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: I'm not sure about my shoes feeling tighter, but I haven't really noticed any puffiness in my legs. To be honest, my main concern is still this cough and getting some relief from it. I've been taking OTC dextromethorphan to help with the coughing, but it's only given me minimal relief so far.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been feeling a bit off, but not in my stomach. The dextromethorphan has actually made me feel a bit woozy and dizzy when I take it, which is why I'm getting a bit anxious about taking more of it for relief. But aside from that, no, I haven't really noticed any stomach issues during the episodes.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: No, actually, I've been feeling a bit off, but not in my stomach. The dextromethorphan has actually made me feel a bit woozy and dizzy when I take it, which is why I'm getting a bit anxious about taking more of it for relief. But aside from that, no, I haven't really noticed any stomach issues during the episodes.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: Yes, I've actually been experiencing a low-grade fever, it's usually around 38.2°C (100.8°F), and it's worse in the evenings. And like I mentioned earlier, I've also had some pretty intense night sweats - for the past 4 nights, I've woken up with my pajamas completely soaked through.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: Yes, actually. Over the past 5 days, I've noticed that my appetite has decreased significantly. I'm not feeling like eating much of anything, which is unusual for me because I usually enjoy food and cooking.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient:
I'm a teacher, so my days are pretty structured. I work with young children, which can be physically demanding at times, but I also make sure to take care of myself. In terms of diet, I try to eat healthy and balanced meals, especially during the week when I'm working. Over the weekend, I might indulge a bit more in social gatherings or try out new recipes. As for exercise, I don't have much time for intense workouts, but I like to stay active by taking short walks with my partner during lunch breaks or on weekends.

I do have some allergies, seasonal allergic rhinitis, which can affect my sinuses and make me feel congested at times. But I've learned to manage it with over-the-counter meds like cetirizine when needed. Other than that, I don't think there's anything out of the ordinary in my daily routine or lifestyle that could be contributing to this cough.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: I'm moderately active, I'd say. As a teacher, I don't have time for intense workouts, but I try to stay active by taking short walks with my partner during lunch breaks or on weekends. It's not regular exercise, per se, but more like gentle movement to keep me going throughout the day.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: A typical day of eating for me is pretty routine. I usually have breakfast around 7-8 am, which can be anything from oatmeal with fruit to scrambled eggs with whole-grain toast. During the week when I'm working, my lunches are often leftovers or salads that I prepare at home the night before. Sometimes, if I have time, I might meet up with colleagues for lunch and we'll grab sandwiches or wraps from a nearby café. In the evenings, I try to cook healthy dinners, like grilled chicken or fish with roasted vegetables, but on weekends when I'm not working, my partner and I might indulge in takeout or try out new recipes together. Overall, I'd say I eat a balanced diet, but over the past 5 days since this cough started, I've definitely noticed that my appetite has decreased significantly.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My mom had asthma when she was younger, and my grandmother had chronic bronchitis. But aside from those conditions, I don't think there are any other significant medical issues in my family that I'm aware of.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: Yes, my mother actually has asthma. She's managed it pretty well over the years with her treatment, but I've always been a bit more cautious about potential respiratory issues because of that.
